title: MacOS volume sharing located at /tmp/<dir> fails with "Error: statfs /tmp/<dir>: no such file or directory"
body:
### Issue Description

Trying to share a volume located at a sub-directory of /tmp/ fails with an error:
Error: statfs /tmp/<dir>: no such file or directory

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. mkdir /tmp/a 
2. podman run -it --rm -v /tmp/a:/mnt alpine /bin/ash
3.


### Describe the results you received

podman run -it --rm -v /tmp/a:/mnt alpine /bin/ash
Error: statfs /tmp/a: no such file or directory

### Describe the results you expected

podman run -it --rm -v /private/tmp/a:/mnt alpine /bin/ash
/ #

### podman info output

comments.body: @baude @ashley-cui PTAL, looks mac specific. Can't reproduce on fedora.
I think it'd be nice to have github teams `macos-maintainers`, `windows-maintainers`, `bsd-maintainers` or something which could be conveniently tagged for non-linux issues/PRs.

Hello all,

I know I already assigned myself to this issue, but I wanted to briefly introduce myself. I'm a computer science student at the University of Texas at Austin, and I'm hoping to work on this issue as part of a final project for a Virtualization course.

Could someone confirm whether this issue is still valid and within scope? Also, if there’s a particular part of the codebase that would be a good starting point. Since /tmp is a symlink for /private/tmp, I'd reckon that something might be going wrong there.

My project timeline runs for the next few weeks, so I'll be actively working on this during that period. I've already been able to reproduce the issue on my MacBook with the steps provided, so I'm hoping to find a lead soon.

Thanks, and I’m looking forward to contributing!

I also have this issue. Podman version 5.7.0:
```
$ podman machine info
host:
    arch: arm64
    currentmachine: podman-machine-default
    defaultmachine: podman-machine-default
    eventsdir: /var/folders/d2/xpykw8px0zl3wx7q3q2zlphw0000gn/T/storage-run-501/podman
    machineconfigdir: /Users/username/.config/containers/podman/machine/applehv
    machineimagedir: /Users/username/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv
    machinestate: Running
    numberofmachines: 1
    os: darwin
    vmtype: applehv
version:
    >>--> apiversion: 5.7.0
    version: 5.7.0
    goversion: go1.25.4
    gitcommit: ""
    builttime: Tue Nov 11 09:51:07 2025
    built: 1762869067
    buildorigin: brew
    osarch: darwin/arm64
    os: darwin
```

Hello all again,

On macOS and Windows, podman uses a virtual machine to run its containers. [When you try to mount a directory to your container, it attempts the mount from this virtual machine (called the remote server), rather than your operating system.](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#:~:text=%28Note%20when%20using%20the%20remote%20client%2C%20including%20Mac%20and%20Windows%20%28excluding%20WSL2%29%20machines%2C%20the%20volumes%20are%20mounted%20from%20the%20remote%20server%2C%20not%20necessarily%20the%20client%20machine%2E%29) This virtual machine can be managed through `podman machine`. Its default config mounts /Users and /private from your filesystem, which is why most standard mounts work out of the box.

For MacOS: Because /tmp is a symlink to /private/tmp, and /private is already mounted by default, you can simply use the canonical path to share volumes
`podman run -it --rm -v /private/tmp/a:/mnt alpine /bin/ash`

For Windows (or other non-mounted directories): If you need to mount a directory that isn't shared by default (like a separate drive or system temp folder), you can modify the podman machine init command:
```
# Example: Mounting a Windows folder
podman machine init -v "C:\Temp:/mnt/temp"
podman machine start
podman run -it --rm -v /mnt/temp:/mnt alpine /bin/ash
```

To my understanding, podman is working "correctly" here.

@Banana-Cultist as you correctly mentioned, when starting a podman machine, some default host folders are bind-mounted so that they are "visible" for the podman service running in the guest Linux VM:
- On Windows `C:/` is mounted in `/mnt/c`
- On macOS `/Users`, `/private`, `/var/folders` are mounted in the guest folders with the same name
- On Linux `$HOME` is mounted as `$HOME`

These lists can be overridden when running `machine init` with the [`--volume` parameter](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-machine-init.1.html#volume-v-source-target-options) but also in the [machine section of `containers.conf`](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/blob/main/common/docs/containers.conf.5.md#machine-table). Except for WSL where the mounted folders are controlled by WSL itself. 

In other words the current issue is mainly a configuration issue. But we can still improve things:
- update the documentation: I don't think the machine mounting mechanism and the defaults are documented. The windows and macos tutorials ([1](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/mac_win_client.md) and [2](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/podman-for-windows.md)) may be a good place to add these informations.
- client side verification: when a user does a `podman run -v <host-folder>:<container-folder>` we can check if `<host-folder>` is in the list of machine mounted folders and provide a clear warning (with a link to the machine volumes docs) if it's not.
- client side mapping: if podman is configured to mount the host `/foo/` under guest `/bar/` then `podman -v /foo/<some-sub-folder>:...` should automatically replace `/foo` with `/bar` (something that we are currently doing on Windows but mapping is hardcoded).
